ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn provides a general waffle class; blueberry-filled variant is a subtype not separately coded. Exact match applied at the waffle level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers waffles generically; blueberry filling is not separately faceted in the available hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for blueberry waffle; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard blueberry waffle weighing approximately 75 g, typically consumed as a snack or light breakfast item.

nutrient_source.title: Waffle (blueberry) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database, Food Composition Record F11100028
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo canonical record F11100028 for Waffle (blueberry). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for blueberry-filled waffle in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 is derived from the closest analogous entry: plain waffle (GI ~76, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Blueberry spread adds sugar but does not meaningfully lower GI relative to the plain waffle base. GL per serving calculated as: GI (76) × carbohydrate per serving (51.38 g × 0.75 serving fraction = 38.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 29.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (23.4 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.67 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, raising blood pressure risk independently of sodium intake.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit blueberry waffle consumption due to the high sugar load (23.4 g/100g) which drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 107 mg/100g is moderate but the sugar-fibre imbalance is the primary concern per AHA dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 23.4 g/100g exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate at 51.38 g/100g with only 1.67 g/100g dietary fibre produces a high glycaemic load (GL ~29 per serving), causing rapid postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Blueberry waffles are not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to a high GI (~76), high sugar (23.4 g/100g), and high glycaemic load (~29 per serving); consumption should be avoided or strictly limited to very small portions with protein or fat co-ingestion to blunt glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate and sugar content (51.38 g carbohydrate, 23.4 g sugar per 100g) with moderate total fat (13.17 g/100g) contributes to dyslipidaemia risk; saturated fat content is not reported but likely present given butter-based batter.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume blueberry waffles only occasionally, as the high sugar and refined carbohydrate content can raise triglycerides and contribute to atherogenic dyslipidaemia; whole-grain or reduced-sugar alternatives are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blueberry waffle is a sweet snack or breakfast item made from a pancake-style batter — typically comprising wheat flour, eggs, milk, butter, and sugar — cooked in a waffle iron to produce a crisp, grid-patterned exterior with a soft, fluffy interior, and filled or layered with blueberry spread. Per 100 g, it provides 345 kcal, 5.55 g protein, 13.17 g fat, 51.38 g carbohydrate, 23.4 g sugar, 1.67 g dietary fibre, and 107 mg sodium, as recorded in the Singapore HPB Foodo database (F11100028). The high sugar content (23.4 g/100g) and high glycaemic index (~76) make this food unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. The blueberry filling contributes modest antioxidant value but is insufficient to offset the high refined sugar and carbohydrate load. Commonly consumed as a snack or light breakfast, it is widely available in Singapore convenience stores, cafes, and bakeries. Allergens include gluten, eggs, and dairy. The food is classified as processed and is not suitable for vegan or gluten-free diets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a blueberry waffle?
What is the sugar content of a blueberry waffle?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo canonical record F11100028 with confidence 0.95. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (plain waffle, University of Sydney GI Database) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate. All mandatory fields are populated.
notes: Fields set to -1 due to data not available in HPB Foodo record F11100028: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this food item as of 2026-04-16. Calcium (31.0 mg/100g) and iron (0.74 mg/100g) are reported by HPB and included. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on plain waffle from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for blueberry-filled waffle was found in approved sources. GL per serving (75 g) = 76 × (51.38 × 0.75) / 100 = 29.3. This food is not a Singaporean-origin dish and therefore multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean cuisine (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply; local_names fields are set to null accordingly.
